memo_markdown:
# BOTTLENECK_NOT_EQUITY

**Decision.** High-voltage subsea power-cable manufacturing and installation are genuine system constraints, but the available evidence does not establish an underpriced, fully diluted per-share cash-flow opportunity versus the MSCI ACWI over the next 24 months.

Mode: `scan`. As of and source cutoff: 2026-07-23. Horizon: 24 months. Universe: globally listed, liquid equities; practical liquidity floor EUR 10m average daily traded value. Benchmark: [MSCI ACWI](https://www.msci.com/indexes/index/892400/msci-acwi-index). Research only; no leverage; no automatic trading.

## Funded demand

The payers are regulated transmission-system operators, governments/CfD counterparties and project developers. Demand is partly committed rather than purely aspirational:

- The UK’s 2026 Allocation Round 7 awarded 8.44 GW of offshore and floating wind, including 8.25 GW of fixed-bottom projects for delivery mainly in 2028–31. Successful projects are offered contracts with the Low Carbon Contracts Company; offshore-wind CfDs run for 20 years. This is credible project funding, although it is not the same as an irrevocable cable order. [UK AR7 results](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/contracts-for-difference-cfd-allocation-round-7-results/contracts-for-difference-allocation-round-7-results-accessible-webpage) and [subsidy description](https://www.gov.uk/government/publications/contracts-for-difference-cfd-allocation-round-7-subsidy-control-transparency-database-estimates/contracts-for-difference-cfd-allocation-round-7-subsidy-control-transparency-database-estimates).
- European policy still targets at least 60 GW offshore wind by 2030 and 300 GW by 2050, but targets are aspirational until auctions, permits, financing and final investment decisions convert them into procurement. [European Commission](https://energy.ec.europa.eu/topics/renewable-energy/offshore-renewable-energy_en).
- Grid demand is broader and more durable than offshore wind alone. The IEA reports about USD 400bn of annual grid investment and says annual investment must rise roughly 50% by 2030; cable costs have nearly doubled since 2019. [IEA Electricity 2026](https://www.iea.org/reports/electricity-2026/grids) and [IEA transmission study](https://www.iea.org/reports/building-the-future-transmission-grid).

Procurement runs from policy/regulated-network plans through auctions, permits and financing to project FID, engineering awards, cable-system contracts and vessel scheduling. Demand fails if auctions do not clear economically, permits or grid approvals slip, financing costs make projects unbankable, or developers cancel/rephase projects.

## Role-neutral system map

```text
funded generation/interconnector or regulated grid plan
→ permits, seabed rights, grid connection and financing [mandatory]
→ route survey and cable-system engineering [mandatory]
→ conductor/insulation/armouring inputs [mandatory]
→ qualified HV HVAC/HVDC manufacture and testing [constraint candidate]
→ accessories, joints and converter/substation interfaces [mandatory]
→ load-out, specialised cable-laying vessel and burial tools [constraint candidate]
→ marine installation, testing and commissioning [mandatory]
→ operating transmission asset
```

Role scan:

- `owner`: qualified factories and specialised installation fleets;
- `unlocker`: extrusion/test equipment, ports and marine engineering;
- `substitute`: alternative cable architectures, additional qualified Asian supply, changed routing or overhead/onshore transmission where feasible;
- `tollbooth`: certification, seabed rights, permits and regulated grid connections;
- `absorber`: qualified producers or vessels with spare compatible slots;
- `public_proxy`: diversified cable groups, offshore contractors and grid-equipment suppliers when the exact constrained asset is private.

No listed exposure is assumed merely because it participates in electrification.

## Constraint proof and duration

The constraint-reality gate passes at theme level. The IEA’s industry survey found growing transmission-component constraints, longer procurement and cable costs nearly twice 2019 levels; it specifically identifies specialised high-voltage subsea demand as adding pressure. [IEA](https://www.iea.org/news/rising-component-prices-and-supply-chain-pressures-are-hindering-the-development-of-transmission-grid-infrastructure). Backlogs and customer awards corroborate tight qualified capacity.

Qualification, tall continuous-production assets, test facilities, project engineering and compatible installation fleets make rapid substitution difficult. Nevertheless, scarcity is not static. New factories, lines and vessels are already entering service: one supplier completed a Norwegian subsea expansion, another is investing approximately EUR 1.3bn in a new factory and vessel expected in 2027, and a third placed a new vessel into service in mid-2026. [Norwegian expansion](https://www.nexans.com/press-releases/nexans-finalizes-expansion-of-world-class-subsea-cable-facility-in-norway-to-address-global-demand/), [2027 expansion](https://www.nkt.com/news-press-releases/we-are-building-the-world-s-largest-high-voltage-offshore-cable-factory), [2026 vessel](https://www.nexans.com/app/uploads/2026/02/2026-02-19-pr-nexans-full-year-2025-earnings.pdf).

Estimated scarcity clock: P10 12 months, P50 30 months, P90 48 months. These are forecasts, not measured fleet-utilisation statistics. The 24-month thesis therefore overlaps a substantial capacity-response phase.

## Security map

After verifying the functional bottleneck, the liquid listed owner/public-proxy universe is:

| Security | Legal entity/listing | Role | Evidence of exposure | Principal issue |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| PRY | Prysmian S.p.A., Milan | owner/absorber | EUR 17bn Transmission backlog; FY25 Transmission Q4 margin 20.9% | diversified group; strong results and backlog are already publicly visible |
| NKT | NKT A/S, Nasdaq Copenhagen | owner/absorber | owns qualified manufacturing and installation assets; EUR 13.5bn Transmission backlog at Q1 2026 | major capex and 2027 capacity response; project phasing makes quarterly FCF volatile |
| NEX | Nexans S.A., Paris | owner/absorber | subsea-driven backlog and multiple cable-laying vessels | 2025 cash flow benefited from unusually high customer advances; expansion raises capex |
| 5802 | Sumitomo Electric Industries, Tokyo | owner/substitute | cable manufacturing exposure | subsea contribution is less pure and not bridged here |
| 010120 | LS ELECTRIC, Korea | public proxy | indirect group association with grid/cable ecosystem | wrong-entity risk: the key cable asset is not demonstrated as wholly owned by this listed security |
| DEME | DEME Group, Brussels | installer/public proxy | marine installation exposure | cable installation is only one part of a diversified contractor |

Issuer evidence: [Prysmian FY25](https://www.prysmian.com/en/investors), [NKT Q1 2026](https://investors.nkt.com/files/Main/23044/4347798/nkt-interim-report-q1-2026.pdf), and [Nexans FY25](https://www.nexans.com/regulated-informations/full-year-2025-financial-results/).

NKT is used as the representative underwriting candidate because its Q1 disclosure verifies the ownership chain most clearly: the listed NKT A/S owns the Transmission business, factory investments and cable-laying assets. It reported EUR 56m average daily turnover in Q1 2026, 53.72m issued shares, and a EUR 13.5bn Transmission backlog.

## Equity capture

The industry constraint has reached reported economics: NKT’s Q1 2026 Transmission revenue was EUR 331m at standard metal prices, EBITDA margin was 15.1%, and good vessel utilisation contributed to profitability. However, revenue declined 8% organically because a major project ramped down. This shows that backlog does not map mechanically to near-term revenue.

A valid per-share bridge would require:

```text
backlog executed within 24 months
× company-performed rather than subcontracted content
× realised project margin
− incremental opex, tax, working capital, interest and maintenance/growth capex
= attributable FCF
÷ fully diluted shares including all contingent dilution
```

That bridge is incomplete. Public disclosures reviewed do not isolate the share of Transmission backlog executed during the horizon, subsea manufacturing versus installation economics, project-specific cancellation/repricing terms, maintenance versus expansion capex, or all contingent diluted shares. Consequently no reproducible subsea-bottleneck FCF-per-share figure is presented. This activates `no_material_revenue_bridge`; the shareholder-rent gate fails even though the physical bottleneck is real.

## Three clocks

| Clock | Estimate | Interpretation |
|---|---:|---|
| Physical scarcity | P10 12 / P50 30 / P90 48 months | Backlogs support persistence, but 2026–27 factories and vessels shorten the tail |
| Company monetisation | 12 months | Existing backlog is producing revenue now, but mix and phasing are volatile |
| Market discovery | 6 months | Record backlogs, margin gains and expansions are already prominent disclosures |
| P50 monetisable runway | 18 months | Positive, but insufficient without a complete per-share bridge and expectation gap |

## Valuation and scenarios

A cutoff-date, independently reproducible market price/enterprise value and consensus set was not obtained. Therefore the expectation-gap gate cannot pass. The following are explicit research assumptions relative to MSCI ACWI, not price targets:

- Bear, 30% probability: −35% relative return if project delays, working-capital reversal and capacity additions compress margins.
- Base, 45%: +10% if backlog execution offsets capex but much of the scarcity rent is already expected.
- Bull, 25%: +45% if execution, vessel utilisation and pricing exceed expectations without dilution.

Probability-weighted relative return is +5.25%, too assumption-dependent to establish investability. No order or position follows from these scenarios.

## Catalysts

- 14 August 2026: NKT H1 report—Transmission execution, cash flow, working capital and investment progress.
- 29 September 2026: NKT investor day—capacity, vessel and addressable-market disclosure.
- 19 November 2026: NKT nine-month report—backlog conversion and margin evidence.
- 2027: scheduled commissioning of new production capacity, second vessel and installation assets—both a revenue catalyst and a scarcity-resolution catalyst.

Dates are disclosed in [NKT Q1 2026](https://investors.nkt.com/files/Main/23044/4347798/nkt-interim-report-q1-2026.pdf).

## Red team: strongest alternative causal explanation

The strongest alternative is that observed backlogs and delays are primarily a coordination and project-bankability problem—not a durable manufacturer/vessel rent pool. Permitting, grid queues, seabed approvals, financing and project repricing can bunch nominal demand into a few procurement windows. Suppliers then book large turnkey backlogs and customer advances, while execution risk, expansion capex and working capital absorb the apparent rent. Simultaneously, incumbent and entrant capacity additions arrive before the nominal project pipeline becomes firm.

This explanation fits three contrary observations: offshore-wind growth forecasts were cut by more than 25% because of cancellations, policy shifts and costs; new manufacturing/vessel capacity is already being commissioned; and one candidate’s Transmission revenue fell despite record backlog. [IEA Renewables 2025](https://www.iea.org/reports/renewables-2025/executive-summary), [NKT Q1 2026](https://investors.nkt.com/files/Main/23044/4347798/nkt-interim-report-q1-2026.pdf).

## Documented negative search and falsifiers

The negative search covered new capacity, new vessels, project cancellation/delay, demand downgrades, customer bargaining/advances, project phasing, financing, working capital and dilution. Contract escape clauses, qualified fleet utilisation and maintenance-capex splits remain unresolved.

Predeclared falsifiers:

1. Reclassify the physical bottleneck if comparable HV subsea procurement lead times or real cable costs fall at least 20% from the 2025 reference for two consecutive reporting periods.
2. Reject the 24-month rent thesis if qualified capacity/vessel additions due by end-2027 exceed contracted demand additions and backlog-to-revenue conversion weakens.
3. Reject shareholder capture if trailing-12-month Transmission margin falls below 12%, or cumulative FCF remains negative after customer advances while growth capex continues.
4. Reject the demand case if awarded/FID offshore-wind and interconnector capacity scheduled through 2030 falls more than 25% from the July 2026 baseline.
5. Keep the security non-investable until a reproducible fully diluted per-share FCF bridge and cutoff-date valuation show positive base-case relative return without requiring the bull case.

## Portfolio fit

Research portfolio: no position. The three European cable owners share the same root drivers—European regulated grid/offshore capex, HVDC architecture, a concentrated TSO/developer customer set, project-execution risk and 2026–27 capacity response. Pairwise causal overlap is therefore high: PRY–NKT 0.85, PRY–NEX 0.85, NKT–NEX 0.90 on a qualitative 0–1 research scale. A three-name basket would diversify company execution but not the thesis root cause.

## Open critical claims

- Comparable global qualified manufacturing capacity, utilisation and vessel-day supply through 2028.
- Project-by-project cancellation, indexation, advance-payment and margin terms.
- Incremental maintenance/growth capex, working capital and tax attributable to subsea capacity.
- Fully diluted share counts including every contingent instrument.
- Cutoff-date valuation and market-implied backlog conversion for each security.
- Whether Asian additions can qualify for European HVDC projects inside the horizon.

Until these are resolved, the correct conclusion is: **industry bottleneck, but not yet an evidenced listed-equity opportunity.**
